games.explanation: Skenes is the best pitcher on the planet and the market is pricing this like that fact alone settles things, but Toronto is the rare lineup built to make him sweat. Skenes has been notably more vulnerable to left-handed bats this year, and the Jays roll out Springer, Guerrero, and Varsho to attack exactly that split. Corbin is a fine punching bag in the other dugout, sure, but a Pirates lineup leaning on Nick Gonzales and a streaky Reynolds is not the offense to take advantage. Getting plus money on the better team in a projected one-run game is the entire argument, and +130 is too generous for a coin flip.

Framber Valdez is the name on the marquee, but the version of him that shows up against righties this year is a lot more mortal than the reputation suggests, and Baltimore happens to run out a lineup full of them. Henderson and Alonso are the kind of right-handed power bats that have turned Valdez's sinker into hard contact all season, and a park that plays generous to the long ball isn't doing him any favors either. Brandon Young is no one's idea of an ace, but the projected score has this within a run, which is not what a -116 price on the other side implies. Getting the better lineup at a near pick'em against a starter the market is still grading on last year's curve is the entire angle.

Wheeler is the better pitcher and nobody's pretending otherwise, but a plus-money dog against an ace is a price about how the other eight innings go, and Cleveland's bullpen plus Philly's middle relief is not the gap the board is selling. Cecconi gets hit, sure, but a 52-degree night with the wind blowing straight in turns Schwarber's barrels into loud outs and shrinks the margin Wheeler usually banks early. Ramirez is the great equalizer here, the rare bat that doesn't care which side the arm comes from, posting a .341 xwOBA against everyone. At +166 you're getting a one-run game priced like a two-run loss, and the late innings are where this ticket cashes.

Ginn's overall line looks tidy enough to justify a near pick'em, but the split underneath it tells a different story: lefties have been turning him into batting practice all year. San Diego happens to roll out a lineup full of them, with Sheets and a switch-hitting Tatis getting to attack from the side where Ginn's barrel rate against balloons to nearly 13%. Giolito isn't anyone's idea of an ace, but he's the steadier arm in a game where the Padres are the clearly better team by Elo and the better matchup on paper. Getting the favorite at a price that implies a coin flip is the part the market hasn't sorted out yet.

At +166 you're getting a one-run game priced like a two-run loss, and the late innings are where this ticket cashes.

Atlanta is the better team, sure, but Grant Holmes is not the guy to enforce that gap, and the market keeps pricing him like he is. He walks nearly an batter every two innings against righties and gets barreled at double-digit rates, which is a tough combination when James Wood is the first lefty-killer he sees with a 56% hard-hit rate against arms like this. Irvin is no prize either, but the sim still spits out a 4.8 to 4.5 game because both starters leak, and one-run finishes are exactly where the runline cashes. Getting a plus-money hook on a coin flip dressed up as a mismatch is the whole reason this number exists.

Ginn's overall line looks tidy enough to justify a near pick'em, but the split underneath it tells a different story: lefties have been turning him into batting practice all year. San Diego happens to roll out a lineup full of them, with Sheets and a switch-hitting Tatis getting to attack from the side where Ginn's barrel rate against balloons to nearly 13%. Giolito isn't anyone's idea of an ace, but he's the steadier arm in a game where the Padres are the clearly better team by Elo and the better matchup on paper. Getting the favorite at a price that implies a coin flip is the part the market hasn't sorted out yet.
